text: We present a case of a forty-five-year-old gentleman with a history of several episodes of alcoholic pancreatitis. He subsequently developed chronic pancreatitis causing persistent pain and requiring enzyme replacement. He recently quit drinking but had persistent symptoms. His current lipase and amylase levels were normal. A CT scan revealed multiple calcified deposits within the pancreas and a dilated pancreatic duct measuring one point four centimeters with a proximal obstructing calcified stone. There was no evidence of masses or pseudocysts.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and exposes the pancreas. A wolf needle is used to identify the pancreatic duct, followed by an ultrasonic scalpel to perform a ductotomy, which is extended distally. A large proximal obstructing stone is retrieved, along with smaller stones. The ductotomy is extended to fifteen centimeters, and additional stones are removed. The duct is extensively irrigated to remove all debris.

The surgeon performs a Roux-en-Y lateral pancreatic jejunostomy, positioning the Roux limb adjacent to the ductotomy. A 15 cm enterotomy is created using an ultrasonic scalpel. Two O silk sutures are placed for the back row, and two O PDS sutures are used for the inner layer anastomosis. The suture transitions to a canal stitch at the corners, carried over to the anterior surface to invert the edges. A second PDS suture is applied to the posterior half, transitioning similarly to complete the anterior anastomosis. A two-oh silk suture completes the second layer of the anterior anastomosis. A stapled jejuno-jejunostomy is performed, and a drain is placed in the lesser sac.

These one and two centimeter stones were the largest removed from the pancreatic duct. The patient had a relatively uncomplicated hospital course. He developed a post-operative ileus but had return of bowel function on post-operative day four. His diet was advanced and his drain amylase and lipase did not show any evidence of a leak. His drain was pulled and he was discharged home on post-operative day five. On twelve month follow up, the patient's symptoms have significantly improved and he remains off narcotics. He now just has mild postprandial pain requiring one to two tabs of Creon per mil.
In conclusion, a laparoscopic lateral pancreatic ojejunostomy is effective in appropriate patients with few complications and a short hospital course.
